title: Fluctuation-induced and quantum effects in nanofluidic transport
authors: Adrien Sutter, Peter Gispert, Baptiste Coquinot, Lydéric Bocquet, Nikita Kavokine
abstract: The hydrodynamic wall has traditionally been considered a featureless object, whose only role is to provide a boundary for fluid flow. Yet, there is now ample evidence that at nanometer scales, liquid flows are sensitive to the wall's internal -- in particular, electronic -- degrees of freedom. Here, after reviewing the experimental evidence for nanoscale liquid-electron couplings, we present the theoretical advances that have allowed for their quantitative understanding. We discuss how a quantum description of the liquid-solid interface reveals the influence of electron dynamics on classical fluid transport, in the form of the fluctuation-induced quantum friction effect. Quantum friction is at the root of liquid-electron coupled transport phenomena, that may be combined into a hydro-electronic transport matrix. We present analytical formulas for the hydro-electronic transport coefficients, that allow for their quantitative estimation in practical cases; we further outline the potential consequences of coupled liquid-electron transport for the water-energy nexus. Fluctuation-induced and quantum effects at liquid-solid interfaces represent an emerging interface between fluid dynamics and condensed-matter physics, and a largely uncharted territory for both theory and experiment.
summary: This research addresses nanoscale fluid transport by developing theoretical frameworks that incorporate electronic fluctuations, leading to the concept of fluctuation-induced quantum friction. By unifying hydrodynamics and electronics into a single matrix, it provides quantitative predictions for coupled transport coefficients relevant to energy applications.
main_problem: The core problem is understanding fluid transport at nanoscale dimensions where liquid flow becomes critically sensitive to the wall's internal electronic degrees of freedom, necessitating a unified description beyond classical continuum models.
main_result: Fluctuation-induced quantum friction effects are predicted to couple hydrodynamic and electronic transport into a single hydro-electronic matrix. The theory establishes quantum fluctuation-dissipation theorems linking structural correlation functions to response functions.
method: The work combines theoretical derivations using Keldysh formalism (Quantum Field Theory) with analysis of experimental results from nanofluidic measurements on various solid substrates.
model_or_system: The system involves liquid confined in nanoscale pores (e.g., CNTs, 2D materials) interacting with solid walls whose electronic structure dictates transport properties. Models range from the PNPS framework to full quantum treatments incorporating electron-phonon and fluid-electron coupling.
key_observables: Hydrodynamic friction coefficient ($\lambda$), ionic conductance ($G_i$), critical confinement length for freezing, and measurable electric currents generated by fluid motion (e.g., phonon drag).
important_parameters: Nanoscale dimensions ($\lesssim 10$ nm), temperature ($T$), quantum mechanical parameters ($\hbar\omega/k_B T$), and the electronic nature of the confining wall (metallic vs. semiconducting).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes that mean-field descriptions fail at small scales, necessitating the inclusion of fluctuating quantum degrees of freedom; approximations are made in deriving analytical formulas from complex field theories.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate relationships such as water friction coefficient vs. CNT radius, and conceptual diagrams showing the Superionic state or energy transfer mechanisms between electrons and water molecules.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first identifying the failure points of classical models (PNPS) at small scales, then introducing quantum effects like fluctuation-induced friction via QFT formalism, and finally benchmarking these theories against diverse experimental measurements on specific nanostructures.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it tackles the interface between fluid dynamics and condensed matter physics using advanced quantum statistical tools (Keldysh formalism), providing a framework for understanding non-equilibrium transport phenomena in confined geometries.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1 Superionic state. (a) Positive ion in the center of a (6,6) carbon nanotube with radius R = 0.4 nm. (b) Resulting Coulomb potential along the tube axis in vacuum (without the nanotube), for a nanopore inside a bulk dielectric material with dielectric constant ϵ = 3 and for a nanotube modeled as an ideal metal. The ideal-metal boundary condition leads to an exponentially screened effective interaction along the tube axis, known as the superionic state (Kondrat and Kornyshev, 2011).

FIG. 2 Ion conductivity and surface charge. (a) Electrical conductance of aqueous KCl solution in fused silica channels of 1015 nm and 70 nm height as a function of KCl concentration, reproduced from Stein et al. (2004). Due to the surface charge on the channel walls, the conductance exhibits a plateau at low concentrations. (b) Electrical conductivity of aqueous KCl solution in single transmembrane boron nitride nanotubes at pH = 5 as a function of KCl concentration. The tubes are about 1 µm long and their radii are (from bottom to top) 40 nm, 29 nm, 22 nm and 15 nm, reproduced from Siria et al. (2013). Due to the surface charge on the nanotube wall, the conductivity plateaus at low...

FIG. 3 Water friction in CNTs. Water friction coefficient λ as a function of CNT radius R from various experiments (Bui et al., 2016; Du et al., 2011; Jue et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2014; Lee and Park, 2016; McGinnis et al., 2018; Secchi et al., 2016a; Yang et al., 2023). All data is from membrane-based assays, except the data in Secchi et al. (2016a), which is from single-tube measurements. For comparison, we also show the friction coefficient predicted by machine-learning-based MD simulations in orange (Thiemann et al., 2022). The color of the data points represents the CNT length, while the symbol indicates the number of walls (circle: multi-walled, triangle: double-walled, square:...

FIG. 4 Coupling of nanofluidic flows and electronic currents. (a) A droplet of ionic solution is pulled along a graphene sheet. (b) The induced voltage in the graphene depends linearly on the droplet velocity. The sign of the voltage depends on the ion species. (a,b) Reproduced from Yin et al. (2014). (c) Coupling of ionic and electronic currents in a CNT. An electrical current in the CNT wall induces an ionic current through the CNT. Conversely, an ionic flow through the CNT induces an electronic current in the CNT wall. (d) Experimental data for the electronic-current-induced ion flow (red) and the ion-flow- induced electronic current (blue). (c,d) Reproduced from Rabinowitz et al....

FIG. 5 Van der Waals force between two extended bodies. The scaling of the van der Waals interaction between two surfaces with their separation distance d is obtained by summing up interaction energies of the individual atoms – by convenience, in a cylindrical geometry.
